---
title: "Your Paper Title"
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
bibliography: references-appendix.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header: preamble-appendix.tex
---

This Internet Appendix is rendered from a separate `.qmd` file
(`internet-appendix.qmd`) with its own bibliography
(`references-appendix.bib`). It demonstrates the same Quarto
features as the main paper but with appendix-specific numbering and
a custom title page. Replace this paragraph with a one- or
two-sentence description of what readers will find in your own
supplement.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# Overview of the Internet Appendix Format {#sec-app-overview}

The title page above is generated by a custom `\maketitle`
redefinition in `preamble-appendix.tex`, not by Quarto's default
title block. The redefinition prints

> *Internet Appendix for* / *Paper Title* / *Authors* /
> *SUPPLEMENTARY RESULTS*

centered on the page, then yields control back to the body. The
paper title comes from the YAML `title:` field --- set it to match
your main paper. Author names are hardcoded in
`preamble-appendix.tex`, so edit them there when you customize the
template.

Sections in this file render as **Appendix A**, **Appendix B**, and
so on, thanks to a `\renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{...}}`
in the same preamble. Tables, figures, and equations follow the
section letter (A.1, A.2, B.1, ...).

# Equations with Appendix Numbering {#sec-app-eq}

A labeled display equation in Appendix B renders as Equation B.1:

$$
\widehat{\beta}_{IA} = (X'X)^{-1} X' y
$$ {#eq-app-baseline}

The cross-reference syntax `@eq-app-baseline` works the same way as
in the main paper. The numbering prefix B comes from the appendix
section letter (this is Appendix B); had the equation been inside
Appendix C, it would render as C.1 instead.

You can chain multiple labeled equations in one section --- the
second becomes B.2, the third B.3, and so on.

$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\bigl(\widehat{\beta}_{IA}\bigr) = \sigma^{2}\, (X'X)^{-1}
$$ {#eq-app-variance}

Reference both: @eq-app-baseline gives the point estimate and
@eq-app-variance gives its variance.

# Theorem Environments and Proofs {#sec-app-theorems}

The same five theorem-style environments are available here:
`theorem`, `proposition`, `lemma`, `definition`, `assumption`, plus
the `proof` div.

::: {.theorem}
*(Generic convergence result.)* If the input series converges to a
finite constant as the sample size grows, the running mean of the
series converges to the same constant.
:::

::: {.proof}
*Sketch.* Apply dominated convergence to the truncated tail of the
series and exchange limit with integration. The full argument
appears in @cooling-handbook2024. $\square$
:::

# Citations and a Separate Bibliography {#sec-app-cites}

The Internet Appendix has its own `bibliography:` YAML field
pointing to `references-appendix.bib`. Citations from the main
paper's `references.bib` are *not* visible here unless you add the
same entry to the appendix bibliography. This separation makes it
easy to keep supplementary-only references (heat-transfer handbooks,
data dictionaries, technical reports) out of the main paper's
reference list.

For example, the appendix cites @cooling-handbook2024 above; the
main paper does not.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
